    paragraph "The Manufacturers Trust Company Building is a commercial building in Midtown Manhattan , New York City. Opened in 1954, it was the first bank building in the United States to be built in the International Style . It was commissioned by the Manufacturers Trust Company and designed by Skidmore, Owing…"

    list
      listitem "... that the tortilla (examples pictured) has been eaten for thousands of years , but it has only been considered a national symbol of Mexico since the 20th century?"

      listitem "... that magician Sterling Dietz used $1,200 in prize earnings from a chess tournament to pay for tuition at a Las Vegas magic school?"

    paragraph "The Ming dynasty continued the use of era names , a system introduced by Emperor Wu of Han in 114 BC, or more formally in 110 BC.     paragraph "Guido van Rossum ( .mw-parser-output .IPA-label-small{font-size:85%}.mw-parser-output .references .IPA-label-small,.mw-parser-output .infobox .IPA-label-small,.mw-parser-output .navbox .IPA-label-small{font-size:100%} Dutch: [ ˈxidoː vɑn ˈrɔsʏm ] ; born 31 January 1956) is a Dutch programmer . He i…"
      link[e142] "[ ˈxidoː vɑn ˈrɔsʏm ]"
      link[e143] "programmer"
      link[e144] "Python programming language"

  region
    heading "Life and education"
    link[e149] "edit"
    paragraph "Van Rossum was born and raised in the Netherlands , where he received a master's degree in mathematics and computer science from the University of Amsterdam in 1982. He received a bronze medal in 1974 in the International Mathematical Olympiad . [ 7 ] His brother, Just van Rossum , is a type design…"
      link[e150] "Netherlands"
      link[e151] "computer science"
      link[e152] "University of Amsterdam"
      link[e153] "International Mathematical Olympiad"
      link[e154] "[ 7 ]"
      link[e155] "Just van Rossum"
      link[e156] "type designer"
      link[e157] "typeface"
      link[e158] "[ 8 ]"
    paragraph "Van Rossum lives in Belmont , California, with his wife, Kim Knapp, [ 9 ] and their son. [ 10 ] [ 11 ] [ 12 ]"
      link[e159] "Belmont"
      link[e160] "[ 9 ]"
      link[e161] "[ 10 ]"
      link[e162] "[ 11 ]"
      link[e163] "[ 12 ]"
  region
    heading "Work"
    link[e164] "edit"
    region
      heading "Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica"
      link[e165] "edit"
      paragraph "While working at the Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica (CWI), Van Rossum wrote and contributed a glob() routine to BSD Unix in 1986 [ 13 ] [ 14 ] and helped develop the ABC programming language . He once stated, "I try to mention ABC's influence because I'm indebted to everything I learned during that…"
        link[e166] "Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica"
        link[e167] "glob()"
        link[e168] "BSD"
        link[e169] "Unix"
        link[e170] "[ 13 ]"
        link[e171] "[ 14 ]"
        link[e172] "ABC programming language"
        link[e173] "[ 15 ]"
        link[e174] "Grail"
        link[e175] "web browser"
        link[e176] "Python"
        link[e177] "HTML"
        link[e178] "[ 16 ]"
      paragraph "He has worked for various research institutes, including the CWI in the Netherlands, the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), and the Corporation for National Research Initiatives (CNRI). In May 2000, he left CNRI along with three other Python core developers to work for tech…"
        link[e179] "National Institute of Standards and Technology"
        link[e180] "Corporation for National Research Initiatives"
        link[e181] "[ 17 ]"
        link[e182] "[ 18 ]"
        link[e183] "Zope"
        link[e184] "[ 19 ]"
    region
      heading "Google"
      link[e185] "edit"
      paragraph "From 2005 to December 2012, Van Rossum worked at Google, where he spent half of his time developing the Python language. At Google, he developed Mondrian, a web-based code review system written in Python and used within the company. He named the software after the Dutch painter Piet Mondrian . [ 20…"
        link[e186] "code review"
        link[e187] "Piet Mondrian"
        link[e188] "[ 20 ]"
        link[e189] "Rietveld"
        link[e190] "Gerrit Rietveld"
        link[e191] "[ 21 ]"
        link[e192] "[ 22 ]"
    region
      heading "Dropbox"
      link[e193] "edit"
      paragraph "In January 2013, Van Rossum started working at the cloud file storage company Dropbox . [ 23 ] [ 24 ]"
        link[e194] "cloud file storage"
        link[e195] "Dropbox"
        link[e196] "[ 23 ]"
        link[e197] "[ 24 ]"
      paragraph "In October 2019, Van Rossum left Dropbox and officially retired. [ 25 ] [ 26 ] [ 27 ]"
        link[e198] "[ 25 ]"
        link[e199] "[ 26 ]"
        link[e200] "[ 27 ]"
    region
      heading "Microsoft"
      link[e201] "edit"
      paragraph "On 12 November 2020 Van Rossum announced that he was coming out of retirement to join the Developer Division at Microsoft . He currently holds the title Distinguished Engineer at Microsoft. [ 28 ] [ 29 ] [ 30 ]"
        link[e202] "Microsoft"
        link[e203] "[ 28 ]"
        link[e204] "[ 29 ]"
        link[e205] "[ 30 ]"
  region
    heading "Python"
    link[e206] "edit"
    note "Main article: Python (programming language)"
      link[e207] "Python (programming language)"
    paragraph "In December 1989, Van Rossum had been looking for a " ' hobby' programming project that would keep [him] occupied during the week around Christmas" as his office was closed when he decided to write an interpreter for a "new scripting language [he] had been thinking about lately: a descendant of ABC…"
      link[e208] "interpreter"
      link[e209] "ABC"
      link[e210] "Unix"
      link[e211] "C"
      link[e212] "Monty Python's Flying Circus"
    paragraph "He has explained that Python's predecessor, ABC , was inspired by SETL , noting that ABC co-developer Lambert Meertens had "spent a year with the SETL group at NYU before coming up with the final ABC design". [ 31 ]"
      link[e213] "ABC"
      link[e214] "SETL"
      link[e215] "Lambert Meertens"
      link[e216] "NYU"
      link[e217] "[ 31 ]"
    paragraph "On 12 July 2018, Van Rossum announced that he would be stepping down from the position of benevolent dictator for life of the Python programming language. [ 32 ]"
      link[e218] "benevolent dictator for life"
      link[e219] "[ 32 ]"
  region
    heading ""Computer Programming for Everybody" proposal"
    link[e220] "edit"
    paragraph "In 1999, Van Rossum submitted a funding proposal to the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA) called "Computer Programming for Everybody", in which he further defined his goals for Python:"
      link[e221] "Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency"
    list
      listitem "An easy and intuitive language just as powerful as major competitors"
      listitem "Open source , so anyone can contribute to its development"
        link[e222] "Open source"
      listitem "Code that is as understandable as plain English"
      listitem "Suitability for everyday tasks, allowing for short development times"
    paragraph "In 2019, Python became the second most popular language on GitHub , the largest source code management website on the internet, after JavaScript . [ 33 ] In 2024 Python became the most used language on GitHub , overtaking JavaScript after a 10-year run as the most used language. [ 34 ] According to…"
      link[e223] "GitHub"
      link[e224] "source code"
      link[e225] "JavaScript"
      link[e226] "[ 33 ]"
      link[e227] "GitHub"
      link[e228] "JavaScript"
      link[e229] "[ 34 ]"
      link[e230] "[ 35 ]"
      link[e231] "TIOBE Programming Community Index"
      link[e232] "[ 36 ]"
  region
    heading "Awards"
    link[e233] "edit"
    list
      listitem "At the 2002 FOSDEM conference in Brussels , Van Rossum received the 2001 Award for the Advancement of Free Software from the Free Software Foundation (FSF) for his work on Python."
        link[e234] "FOSDEM"
        link[e235] "Brussels"
        link[e236] "Award for the Advancement of Free Software"
        link[e237] "Free Software Foundation"
      listitem "In May 2003, he received a NLUUG Award. [ 37 ]"
        link[e238] "NLUUG"
        link[e239] "[ 37 ]"
      listitem "In 2006, he was recognized as an ACM Distinguished Engineer ."
        link[e240] "ACM Distinguished Engineer"
      listitem "In 2018, he was made a Fellow of the Computer History museum . [ 38 ]"
        link[e241] "Computer History museum"
        link[e242] "[ 38 ]"
      listitem "In 2019, he was awarded the honorary title of Dijkstra Fellow by CWI . [ 39 ]"
        link[e243] "CWI"
        link[e244] "[ 39 ]"
      listitem "In 2023, he was awarded the C&C Prize by NEC Corporation for developing the Python programming language. [ 40 ]"
        link[e245] "C&C Prize"
        link[e246] "[ 40 ]"
  region

    paragraph "Guido van Rossum ( .mw-parser-output .IPA-label-small{font-size:85%}.mw-parser-output .references .IPA-label-small,.mw-parser-output .infobox .IPA-label-small,.mw-parser-output .navbox .IPA-label-small{font-size:100%} Dutch: [ ˈxidoː vɑn ˈrɔsʏm ] ; born 31 January 1956) is a Dutch programmer .   region
    heading "Life and education"
    link[e149] "edit"
    paragraph "Van Rossum was born and raised in the Netherlands , where he received a master's degree in mathematics and computer science from the University of Amsterdam in 1982. He received a bronze medal in 1974 in the International Mathematical Olympiad . [ 7 ] His brother, Just van Rossum , is a type design…"
      link[e150] "Netherlands"
      link[e151] "computer science"
      link[e152] "University of Amsterdam"
      link[e153] "International Mathematical Olympiad"
      link[e154] "[ 7 ]"
      link[e155] "Just van Rossum"
      link[e156] "type designer"
      link[e157] "typeface"
      link[e158] "[ 8 ]"
    paragraph "Van Rossum lives in Belmont , California, with his wife, Kim Knapp, [ 9 ] and their son. [ 10 ] [ 11 ] [ 12 ]"
      link[e159] "Belmont"
      link[e160] "[ 9 ]"
      link[e161] "[ 10 ]"
      link[e162] "[ 11 ]"
      link[e163] "[ 12 ]"
  region
    heading "Work"
    link[e164] "edit"
    region
      heading "Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica"
      link[e165] "edit"
      paragraph "While working at the Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica (CWI), Van Rossum wrote and contributed a glob() routine to BSD Unix in 1986 [ 13 ] [ 14 ] and helped develop the ABC programming language . He once stated, "I try to mention ABC's influence because I'm indebted to everything I learned during that…"
        link[e166] "Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica"
        link[e167] "glob()"
        link[e168] "BSD"
        link[e169] "Unix"
        link[e170] "[ 13 ]"
        link[e171] "[ 14 ]"
        link[e172] "ABC programming language"
        link[e173] "[ 15 ]"
        link[e174] "Grail"
        link[e175] "web browser"
        link[e176] "Python"
        link[e177] "HTML"
        link[e178] "[ 16 ]"
      paragraph "He has worked for various research institutes, including the CWI in the Netherlands, the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), and the Corporation for National Research Initiatives (CNRI). In May 2000, he left CNRI along with three other Python core developers to work for tech…"
        link[e179] "National Institute of Standards and Technology"
        link[e180] "Corporation for National Research Initiatives"
        link[e181] "[ 17 ]"
        link[e182] "[ 18 ]"
        link[e183] "Zope"
        link[e184] "[ 19 ]"
    region
      heading "Google"
      link[e185] "edit"
      paragraph "From 2005 to December 2012, Van Rossum worked at Google, where he spent half of his time developing the Python language. At Google, he developed Mondrian, a web-based code review system written in Python and used within the company. He named the software after the Dutch painter Piet Mondrian . [ 20…"
        link[e186] "code review"
        link[e187] "Piet Mondrian"
        link[e188] "[ 20 ]"
        link[e189] "Rietveld"
        link[e190] "Gerrit Rietveld"
        link[e191] "[ 21 ]"
        link[e192] "[ 22 ]"
    region
      heading "Dropbox"
      link[e193] "edit"
      paragraph "In January 2013, Van Rossum started working at the cloud file storage company Dropbox . [ 23 ] [ 24 ]"
        link[e194] "cloud file storage"
        link[e195] "Dropbox"
        link[e196] "[ 23 ]"
        link[e197] "[ 24 ]"
      paragraph "In October 2019, Van Rossum left Dropbox and officially retired. [ 25 ] [ 26 ] [ 27 ]"
        link[e198] "[ 25 ]"
        link[e199] "[ 26 ]"
        link[e200] "[ 27 ]"
    region
      heading "Microsoft"
      link[e201] "edit"
      paragraph "On 12 November 2020 Van Rossum announced that he was coming out of retirement to join the Developer Division at Microsoft . He currently holds the title Distinguished Engineer at Microsoft. [ 28 ] [ 29 ] [ 30 ]"
        link[e202] "Microsoft"
        link[e203] "[ 28 ]"
        link[e204] "[ 29 ]"
        link[e205] "[ 30 ]"
  region
    heading "Python"
    link[e206] "edit"
    note "Main article: Python (programming language)"
      link[e207] "Python (programming language)"
    paragraph "In December 1989, Van Rossum had been looking for a " ' hobby' programming project that would keep [him] occupied during the week around Christmas" as his office was closed when he decided to write an interpreter for a "new scripting language [he] had been thinking about lately: a descendant of ABC…"
      link[e208] "interpreter"
      link[e209] "ABC"
      link[e210] "Unix"
      link[e211] "C"
      link[e212] "Monty Python's Flying Circus"
    paragraph "He has explained that Python's predecessor, ABC , was inspired by SETL , noting that ABC co-developer Lambert Meertens had "spent a year with the SETL group at NYU before coming up with the final ABC design". [ 31 ]"
      link[e213] "ABC"
      link[e214] "SETL"
      link[e215] "Lambert Meertens"
      link[e216] "NYU"
      link[e217] "[ 31 ]"
    paragraph "On 12 July 2018, Van Rossum announced that he would be stepping down from the position of benevolent dictator for life of the Python programming language. [ 32 ]"
      link[e218] "benevolent dictator for life"
      link[e219] "[ 32 ]"
  region
    heading ""Computer Programming for Everybody" proposal"
    link[e220] "edit"
    paragraph "In 1999, Van Rossum submitted a funding proposal to the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA) called "Computer Programming for Everybody", in which he further defined his goals for Python:"
      link[e221] "Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency"
    list
      listitem "An easy and intuitive language just as powerful as major competitors"
      listitem "Open source , so anyone can contribute to its development"
        link[e222] "Open source"
      listitem "Code that is as understandable as plain English"
      listitem "Suitability for everyday tasks, allowing for short development times"
    paragraph "In 2019, Python became the second most popular language on GitHub , the largest source code management website on the internet, after JavaScript . [ 33 ] In 2024 Python became the most used language on GitHub , overtaking JavaScript after a 10-year run as the most used language. [ 34 ] According to…"
      link[e223] "GitHub"
      link[e224] "source code"
      link[e225] "JavaScript"
      link[e226] "[ 33 ]"
      link[e227] "GitHub"
      link[e228] "JavaScript"
      link[e229] "[ 34 ]"
      link[e230] "[ 35 ]"
      link[e231] "TIOBE Programming Community Index"
      link[e232] "[ 36 ]"
  region
    heading "Awards"
    link[e233] "edit"
    list
      listitem "At the 2002 FOSDEM conference in Brussels , Van Rossum received the 2001 Award for the Advancement of Free Software from the Free Software Foundation (FSF) for his work on Python."
        link[e234] "FOSDEM"
        link[e235] "Brussels"
        link[e236] "Award for the Advancement of Free Software"
        link[e237] "Free Software Foundation"
      listitem "In May 2003, he received a NLUUG Award. [ 37 ]"
        link[e238] "NLUUG"
        link[e239] "[ 37 ]"
      listitem "In 2006, he was recognized as an ACM Distinguished Engineer ."
        link[e240] "ACM Distinguished Engineer"
      listitem "In 2018, he was made a Fellow of the Computer History museum . [ 38 ]"
        link[e241] "Computer History museum"
        link[e242] "[ 38 ]"
      listitem "In 2019, he was awarded the honorary title of Dijkstra Fellow by CWI . [ 39 ]"
        link[e243] "CWI"
        link[e244] "[ 39 ]"
      listitem "In 2023, he was awarded the C&C Prize by NEC Corporation for developing the Python programming language. [ 40 ]"
        link[e245] "C&C Prize"
        link[e246] "[ 40 ]"

  region
    heading "Life and education"
    link[e149] "edit"
    paragraph "Van Rossum was born and raised in the Netherlands , where he received a master's degree in mathematics and computer science from the University of Amsterdam in 1982. He received a bronze medal in 1974 in the International Mathematical Olympiad . [ 7 ] His brother, Just van Rossum , is a type design…"
      link[e150] "Netherlands"
      link[e151] "computer science"
      link[e152] "University of Amsterdam"
      link[e153] "International Mathematical Olympiad"
      link[e154] "[ 7 ]"
      link[e155] "Just van Rossum"
      link[e156] "type designer"
      link[e157] "typeface"
      link[e158] "[ 8 ]"
    paragraph "Van Rossum lives in Belmont , California, with his wife, Kim Knapp, [ 9 ] and their son. [ 10 ] [ 11 ] [ 12 ]"
      link[e159] "Belmont"
      link[e160] "[ 9 ]"
      link[e161] "[ 10 ]"
      link[e162] "[ 11 ]"
      link[e163] "[ 12 ]"
  region
    heading "Work"
    link[e164] "edit"
    region
      heading "Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica"
      link[e165] "edit"
      paragraph "While working at the Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica (CWI), Van Rossum wrote and contributed a glob() routine to BSD Unix in 1986 [ 13 ] [ 14 ] and helped develop the ABC programming language . He once stated, "I try to mention ABC's influence because I'm indebted to everything I learned during that…"
        link[e166] "Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica"
        link[e167] "glob()"
        link[e168] "BSD"
        link[e169] "Unix"
        link[e170] "[ 13 ]"
        link[e171] "[ 14 ]"
        link[e172] "ABC programming language"
        link[e173] "[ 15 ]"
        link[e174] "Grail"
        link[e175] "web browser"
        link[e176] "Python"
        link[e177] "HTML"
        link[e178] "[ 16 ]"
      paragraph "He has worked for various research institutes, including the CWI in the Netherlands, the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), and the Corporation for National Research Initiatives (CNRI). In May 2000, he left CNRI along with three other Python core developers to work for tech…"
        link[e179] "National Institute of Standards and Technology"
        link[e180] "Corporation for National Research Initiatives"
        link[e181] "[ 17 ]"
        link[e182] "[ 18 ]"
        link[e183] "Zope"
        link[e184] "[ 19 ]"
    region
      heading "Google"
      link[e185] "edit"
      paragraph "From 2005 to December 2012, Van Rossum worked at Google, where he spent half of his time developing the Python language. At Google, he developed Mondrian, a web-based code review system written in Python and used within the company. He named the software after the Dutch painter Piet Mondrian . [ 20…"
        link[e186] "code review"
        link[e187] "Piet Mondrian"
        link[e188] "[ 20 ]"
        link[e189] "Rietveld"
        link[e190] "Gerrit Rietveld"
        link[e191] "[ 21 ]"
        link[e192] "[ 22 ]"
    region
      heading "Dropbox"
      link[e193] "edit"
      paragraph "In January 2013, Van Rossum started working at the cloud file storage company Dropbox . [ 23 ] [ 24 ]"
        link[e194] "cloud file storage"
        link[e195] "Dropbox"
        link[e196] "[ 23 ]"
        link[e197] "[ 24 ]"
      paragraph "In October 2019, Van Rossum left Dropbox and officially retired. [ 25 ] [ 26 ] [ 27 ]"
        link[e198] "[ 25 ]"
        link[e199] "[ 26 ]"
        link[e200] "[ 27 ]"
    region
      heading "Microsoft"
      link[e201] "edit"
      paragraph "On 12 November 2020 Van Rossum announced that he was coming out of retirement to join the Developer Division at Microsoft . He currently holds the title Distinguished Engineer at Microsoft. [ 28 ] [ 29 ] [ 30 ]"
        link[e202] "Microsoft"
        link[e203] "[ 28 ]"
        link[e204] "[ 29 ]"
        link[e205] "[ 30 ]"
  region
    heading "Python"
    link[e206] "edit"
    note "Main article: Python (programming language)"
      link[e207] "Python (programming language)"
    paragraph "In December 1989, Van Rossum had been looking for a " ' hobby' programming project that would keep [him] occupied during the week around Christmas" as his office was closed when he decided to write an interpreter for a "new scripting language [he] had been thinking about lately: a descendant of ABC…"
      link[e208] "interpreter"
      link[e209] "ABC"
      link[e210] "Unix"
      link[e211] "C"
      link[e212] "Monty Python's Flying Circus"
    paragraph "He has explained that Python's predecessor, ABC , was inspired by SETL , noting that ABC co-developer Lambert Meertens had "spent a year with the SETL group at NYU before coming up with the final ABC design". [ 31 ]"
      link[e213] "ABC"
      link[e214] "SETL"
      link[e215] "Lambert Meertens"
      link[e216] "NYU"
      link[e217] "[ 31 ]"
    paragraph "On 12 July 2018, Van Rossum announced that he would be stepping down from the position of benevolent dictator for life of the Python programming language. [ 32 ]"
      link[e218] "benevolent dictator for life"
      link[e219] "[ 32 ]"
  region
    heading ""Computer Programming for Everybody" proposal"
    link[e220] "edit"
    paragraph "In 1999, Van Rossum submitted a funding proposal to the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA) called "Computer Programming for Everybody", in which he further defined his goals for Python:"
      link[e221] "Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency"
    list
      listitem "An easy and intuitive language just as powerful as major competitors"
      listitem "Open source , so anyone can contribute to its development"
        link[e222] "Open source"
      listitem "Code that is as understandable as plain English"
      listitem "Suitability for everyday tasks, allowing for short development times"
    paragraph "In 2019, Python became the second most popular language on GitHub , the largest source code management website on the internet, after JavaScript . [ 33 ] In 2024 Python became the most used language on GitHub , overtaking JavaScript after a 10-year run as the most used language. [ 34 ] According to…"
      link[e223] "GitHub"
      link[e224] "source code"
      link[e225] "JavaScript"
      link[e226] "[ 33 ]"

      link[e228] "JavaScript"

      link[e230] "[ 35 ]"

      link[e232] "[ 36 ]"
  region
    heading "Awards"
    link[e233] "edit"
    list
      listitem "At the 2002 FOSDEM conference in Brussels , Van Rossum received the 2001 Award for the Advancement of Free Software from the Free Software Foundation (FSF) for his work on Python."
        link[e234] "FOSDEM"
        link[e235] "Brussels"
        link[e236] "Award for the Advancement of Free Software"
        link[e237] "Free Software Foundation"
      listitem "In May 2003, he received a NLUUG Award. [ 37 ]"
        link[e238] "NLUUG"
        link[e239] "[ 37 ]"
      listitem "In 2006, he was recognized as an ACM Distinguished Engineer ."
        link[e240] "ACM Distinguished Engineer"
      listitem "In 2018, he was made a Fellow of the Computer History museum . [ 38 ]"
        link[e241] "Computer History museum"
        link[e242] "[ 38 ]"
      listitem "In 2019, he was awarded the honorary title of Dijkstra Fellow by CWI . [ 39 ]"
        link[e243] "CWI"
        link[e244] "[ 39 ]"
      listitem "In 2023, he was awarded the C&C Prize by NEC Corporation for developing the Python programming language. [ 40 ]"
        link[e245] "C&C Prize"
        link[e246] "[ 40 ]"

    paragraph "Van Rossum was born and raised in the Netherlands , where he received a master's degree in mathematics and computer science from the University of Amsterdam in 1982.     paragraph "Van Rossum lives in Belmont , California, with his wife, Kim Knapp, [ 9 ] and their son. [ 10 ] [ 11 ] [ 12 ]"

      link[e160] "[ 9 ]"
      link[e161] "[ 10 ]"
      link[e162] "[ 11 ]"
      link[e163] "[ 12 ]"
  region
    heading "Work"
    link[e164] "edit"
    region
      heading "Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica"
      link[e165] "edit"
      paragraph "While working at the Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica (CWI), Van Rossum wrote and contributed a glob() routine to BSD Unix in 1986 [ 13 ] [ 14 ] and helped develop the ABC programming language . He once stated, "I try to mention ABC's influence because I'm indebted to everything I learned during that…"
        link[e166] "Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica"
        link[e167] "glob()"
        link[e168] "BSD"
        link[e169] "Unix"
        link[e170] "[ 13 ]"
        link[e171] "[ 14 ]"
        link[e172] "ABC programming language"
        link[e173] "[ 15 ]"
        link[e174] "Grail"
        link[e175] "web browser"
        link[e176] "Python"
        link[e177] "HTML"
        link[e178] "[ 16 ]"
      paragraph "He has worked for various research institutes, including the CWI in the Netherlands, the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), and the Corporation for National Research Initiatives (CNRI). In May 2000, he left CNRI along with three other Python core developers to work for tech…"
        link[e179] "National Institute of Standards and Technology"
        link[e180] "Corporation for National Research Initiatives"
        link[e181] "[ 17 ]"
        link[e182] "[ 18 ]"
        link[e183] "Zope"
        link[e184] "[ 19 ]"
    region
      heading "Google"
      link[e185] "edit"
      paragraph "From 2005 to December 2012, Van Rossum worked at Google, where he spent half of his time developing the Python language. At Google, he developed Mondrian, a web-based code review system written in Python and used within the company. He named the software after the Dutch painter Piet Mondrian . [ 20…"
        link[e186] "code review"
        link[e187] "Piet Mondrian"
        link[e188] "[ 20 ]"
        link[e189] "Rietveld"
        link[e190] "Gerrit Rietveld"
        link[e191] "[ 21 ]"
        link[e192] "[ 22 ]"
    region
      heading "Dropbox"
      link[e193] "edit"
      paragraph "In January 2013, Van Rossum started working at the cloud file storage company Dropbox . [ 23 ] [ 24 ]"
        link[e194] "cloud file storage"
        link[e195] "Dropbox"
        link[e196] "[ 23 ]"
        link[e197] "[ 24 ]"
      paragraph "In October 2019, Van Rossum left Dropbox and officially retired. [ 25 ] [ 26 ] [ 27 ]"
        link[e198] "[ 25 ]"
        link[e199] "[ 26 ]"
        link[e200] "[ 27 ]"
    region
      heading "Microsoft"
      link[e201] "edit"
      paragraph "On 12 November 2020 Van Rossum announced that he was coming out of retirement to join the Developer Division at Microsoft . He currently holds the title Distinguished Engineer at Microsoft. [ 28 ] [ 29 ] [ 30 ]"
        link[e202] "Microsoft"
        link[e203] "[ 28 ]"
        link[e204] "[ 29 ]"
        link[e205] "[ 30 ]"
  region
    heading "Python"
    link[e206] "edit"
    note "Main article: Python (programming language)"
      link[e207] "Python (programming language)"
    paragraph "In December 1989, Van Rossum had been looking for a " ' hobby' programming project that would keep [him] occupied during the week around Christmas" as his office was closed when he decided to write an interpreter for a "new scripting language [he] had been thinking about lately: a descendant of ABC…"
      link[e208] "interpreter"
      link[e209] "ABC"
      link[e210] "Unix"
      link[e211] "C"
      link[e212] "Monty Python's Flying Circus"
    paragraph "He has explained that Python's predecessor, ABC , was inspired by SETL , noting that ABC co-developer Lambert Meertens had "spent a year with the SETL group at NYU before coming up with the final ABC design". [ 31 ]"
      link[e213] "ABC"
      link[e214] "SETL"
      link[e215] "Lambert Meertens"
      link[e216] "NYU"
      link[e217] "[ 31 ]"
    paragraph "On 12 July 2018, Van Rossum announced that he would be stepping down from the position of benevolent dictator for life of the Python programming language. [ 32 ]"
      link[e218] "benevolent dictator for life"
      link[e219] "[ 32 ]"
  region
    heading ""Computer Programming for Everybody" proposal"
    link[e220] "edit"
    paragraph "In 1999, Van Rossum submitted a funding proposal to the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA) called "Computer Programming for Everybody", in which he further defined his goals for Python:"
      link[e221] "Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency"
    list
      listitem "An easy and intuitive language just as powerful as major competitors"
      listitem "Open source , so anyone can contribute to its development"
        link[e222] "Open source"
      listitem "Code that is as understandable as plain English"
      listitem "Suitability for everyday tasks, allowing for short development times"
    paragraph "In 2019, Python became the second most popular language on GitHub , the largest source code management website on the internet, after JavaScript . [ 33 ] In 2024 Python became the most used language on GitHub , overtaking JavaScript after a 10-year run as the most used language. [ 34 ] According to…"
      link[e223] "GitHub"
      link[e224] "source code"
      link[e225] "JavaScript"
      link[e226] "[ 33 ]"
      link[e227] "GitHub"
      link[e228] "JavaScript"
      link[e229] "[ 34 ]"
      link[e230] "[ 35 ]"
      link[e231] "TIOBE Programming Community Index"
      link[e232] "[ 36 ]"
  region
    heading "Awards"
    link[e233] "edit"
    list
      listitem "At the 2002 FOSDEM conference in Brussels , Van Rossum received the 2001 Award for the Advancement of Free Software from the Free Software Foundation (FSF) for his work on Python."
        link[e234] "FOSDEM"
        link[e235] "Brussels"
        link[e236] "Award for the Advancement of Free Software"
        link[e237] "Free Software Foundation"
      listitem "In May 2003, he received a NLUUG Award. [ 37 ]"
        link[e238] "NLUUG"
        link[e239] "[ 37 ]"
      listitem "In 2006, he was recognized as an ACM Distinguished Engineer ."
        link[e240] "ACM Distinguished Engineer"
      listitem "In 2018, he was made a Fellow of the Computer History museum . [ 38 ]"
        link[e241] "Computer History museum"
        link[e242] "[ 38 ]"
      listitem "In 2019, he was awarded the honorary title of Dijkstra Fellow by CWI . [ 39 ]"
        link[e243] "CWI"
        link[e244] "[ 39 ]"
      listitem "In 2023, he was awarded the C&C Prize by NEC Corporation for developing the Python programming language. [ 40 ]"
        link[e245] "C&C Prize"
        link[e246] "[ 40 ]"

    paragraph "Guido van Rossum ( .mw-parser-output .IPA-label-small{font-size:85%}.mw-parser-output .references .IPA-label-small,.mw-parser-output .infobox .IPA-label-small,.mw-parser-output .navbox .IPA-label-small{font-size:100%} Dutch: [ ˈxidoː vɑn ˈrɔsʏm ] ; born 31 January 1956) is a Dutch programmer .   region
    heading "Life and education"
    link[e149] "edit"
    paragraph "Van Rossum was born and raised in the Netherlands , where he received a master's degree in mathematics and computer science from the University of Amsterdam in 1982. He received a bronze medal in 1974 in the International Mathematical Olympiad . [ 7 ] His brother, Just van Rossum , is a type design…"
      link[e150] "Netherlands"
      link[e151] "computer science"
      link[e152] "University of Amsterdam"
      link[e153] "International Mathematical Olympiad"
      link[e154] "[ 7 ]"
      link[e155] "Just van Rossum"
      link[e156] "type designer"
      link[e157] "typeface"
      link[e158] "[ 8 ]"
    paragraph "Van Rossum lives in Belmont , California, with his wife, Kim Knapp, [ 9 ] and their son. [ 10 ] [ 11 ] [ 12 ]"
      link[e159] "Belmont"
      link[e160] "[ 9 ]"
      link[e161] "[ 10 ]"
      link[e162] "[ 11 ]"
      link[e163] "[ 12 ]"
  region
    heading "Work"
    link[e164] "edit"
    region
      heading "Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica"
      link[e165] "edit"
      paragraph "While working at the Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica (CWI), Van Rossum wrote and contributed a glob() routine to BSD Unix in 1986 [ 13 ] [ 14 ] and helped develop the ABC programming language . He once stated, "I try to mention ABC's influence because I'm indebted to everything I learned during that…"
        link[e166] "Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica"
        link[e167] "glob()"
        link[e168] "BSD"
        link[e169] "Unix"
        link[e170] "[ 13 ]"
        link[e171] "[ 14 ]"
        link[e172] "ABC programming language"
        link[e173] "[ 15 ]"
        link[e174] "Grail"
        link[e175] "web browser"
        link[e176] "Python"
        link[e177] "HTML"
        link[e178] "[ 16 ]"
      paragraph "He has worked for various research institutes, including the CWI in the Netherlands, the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), and the Corporation for National Research Initiatives (CNRI). In May 2000, he left CNRI along with three other Python core developers to work for tech…"
        link[e179] "National Institute of Standards and Technology"
        link[e180] "Corporation for National Research Initiatives"
        link[e181] "[ 17 ]"
        link[e182] "[ 18 ]"
        link[e183] "Zope"
        link[e184] "[ 19 ]"
    region
      heading "Google"
      link[e185] "edit"
      paragraph "From 2005 to December 2012, Van Rossum worked at Google, where he spent half of his time developing the Python language. At Google, he developed Mondrian, a web-based code review system written in Python and used within the company. He named the software after the Dutch painter Piet Mondrian . [ 20…"
        link[e186] "code review"
        link[e187] "Piet Mondrian"
        link[e188] "[ 20 ]"
        link[e189] "Rietveld"
        link[e190] "Gerrit Rietveld"
        link[e191] "[ 21 ]"
        link[e192] "[ 22 ]"
    region
      heading "Dropbox"
      link[e193] "edit"
      paragraph "In January 2013, Van Rossum started working at the cloud file storage company Dropbox . [ 23 ] [ 24 ]"
        link[e194] "cloud file storage"
        link[e195] "Dropbox"
        link[e196] "[ 23 ]"
        link[e197] "[ 24 ]"
      paragraph "In October 2019, Van Rossum left Dropbox and officially retired. [ 25 ] [ 26 ] [ 27 ]"
        link[e198] "[ 25 ]"
        link[e199] "[ 26 ]"
        link[e200] "[ 27 ]"
    region
      heading "Microsoft"
      link[e201] "edit"
      paragraph "On 12 November 2020 Van Rossum announced that he was coming out of retirement to join the Developer Division at Microsoft . He currently holds the title Distinguished Engineer at Microsoft. [ 28 ] [ 29 ] [ 30 ]"
        link[e202] "Microsoft"
        link[e203] "[ 28 ]"
        link[e204] "[ 29 ]"
        link[e205] "[ 30 ]"
  region
    heading "Python"
    link[e206] "edit"
    note "Main article: Python (programming language)"
      link[e207] "Python (programming language)"
    paragraph "In December 1989, Van Rossum had been looking for a " ' hobby' programming project that would keep [him] occupied during the week around Christmas" as his office was closed when he decided to write an interpreter for a "new scripting language [he] had been thinking about lately: a descendant of ABC…"
      link[e208] "interpreter"
      link[e209] "ABC"
      link[e210] "Unix"
      link[e211] "C"
      link[e212] "Monty Python's Flying Circus"
    paragraph "He has explained that Python's predecessor, ABC , was inspired by SETL , noting that ABC co-developer Lambert Meertens had "spent a year with the SETL group at NYU before coming up with the final ABC design". [ 31 ]"
      link[e213] "ABC"
      link[e214] "SETL"
      link[e215] "Lambert Meertens"
      link[e216] "NYU"
      link[e217] "[ 31 ]"
    paragraph "On 12 July 2018, Van Rossum announced that he would be stepping down from the position of benevolent dictator for life of the Python programming language. [ 32 ]"
      link[e218] "benevolent dictator for life"
      link[e219] "[ 32 ]"
  region
    heading ""Computer Programming for Everybody" proposal"
    link[e220] "edit"
    paragraph "In 1999, Van Rossum submitted a funding proposal to the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA) called "Computer Programming for Everybody", in which he further defined his goals for Python:"
      link[e221] "Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency"
    list
      listitem "An easy and intuitive language just as powerful as major competitors"
      listitem "Open source , so anyone can contribute to its development"
        link[e222] "Open source"
      listitem "Code that is as understandable as plain English"
      listitem "Suitability for everyday tasks, allowing for short development times"
    paragraph "In 2019, Python became the second most popular language on GitHub , the largest source code management website on the internet, after JavaScript . [ 33 ] In 2024 Python became the most used language on GitHub , overtaking JavaScript after a 10-year run as the most used language. [ 34 ] According to…"

      listitem "... that the tortilla (examples pictured) has been eaten for thousands of years , but it has only been considered a national symbol of Mexico since the 20th century?"

    paragraph "Guido van Rossum ( .mw-parser-output .IPA-label-small{font-size:85%}.mw-parser-output .references .IPA-label-small,.mw-parser-output .infobox .IPA-label-small,.mw-parser-output .navbox .IPA-label-small{font-size:100%} Dutch: [ ˈxidoː vɑn ˈrɔsʏm ] ; born 31 January 1956) is a Dutch programmer . He i…"
      link[e142] "[ ˈxidoː vɑn ˈrɔsʏm ]"
      link[e143] "programmer"
      link[e144] "Python programming language"

  region
    heading "Life and education"
    link[e149] "edit"
    paragraph "Van Rossum was born and raised in the Netherlands , where he received a master's degree in mathematics and computer science from the University of Amsterdam in 1982. He received a bronze medal in 1974 in the International Mathematical Olympiad . [ 7 ] His brother, Just van Rossum , is a type design…"
      link[e150] "Netherlands"
      link[e151] "computer science"
      link[e152] "University of Amsterdam"
      link[e153] "International Mathematical Olympiad"
      link[e154] "[ 7 ]"
      link[e155] "Just van Rossum"
      link[e156] "type designer"
      link[e157] "typeface"
      link[e158] "[ 8 ]"
    paragraph "Van Rossum lives in Belmont , California, with his wife, Kim Knapp, [ 9 ] and their son. [ 10 ] [ 11 ] [ 12 ]"
      link[e159] "Belmont"
      link[e160] "[ 9 ]"
      link[e161] "[ 10 ]"
      link[e162] "[ 11 ]"
      link[e163] "[ 12 ]"
  region
    heading "Work"
    link[e164] "edit"
    region
      heading "Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica"
      link[e165] "edit"
      paragraph "While working at the Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica (CWI), Van Rossum wrote and contributed a glob() routine to BSD Unix in 1986 [ 13 ] [ 14 ] and helped develop the ABC programming language . He once stated, "I try to mention ABC's influence because I'm indebted to everything I learned during that…"
        link[e166] "Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica"
        link[e167] "glob()"
        link[e168] "BSD"
        link[e169] "Unix"
        link[e170] "[ 13 ]"
        link[e171] "[ 14 ]"
        link[e172] "ABC programming language"
        link[e173] "[ 15 ]"
        link[e174] "Grail"
        link[e175] "web browser"
        link[e176] "Python"
        link[e177] "HTML"
        link[e178] "[ 16 ]"
      paragraph "He has worked for various research institutes, including the CWI in the Netherlands, the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), and the Corporation for National Research Initiatives (CNRI). In May 2000, he left CNRI along with three other Python core developers to work for tech…"
        link[e179] "National Institute of Standards and Technology"
        link[e180] "Corporation for National Research Initiatives"
        link[e181] "[ 17 ]"
        link[e182] "[ 18 ]"
        link[e183] "Zope"
        link[e184] "[ 19 ]"
    region
      heading "Google"
      link[e185] "edit"
      paragraph "From 2005 to December 2012, Van Rossum worked at Google, where he spent half of his time developing the Python language. At Google, he developed Mondrian, a web-based code review system written in Python and used within the company. He named the software after the Dutch painter Piet Mondrian . [ 20…"
        link[e186] "code review"
        link[e187] "Piet Mondrian"
        link[e188] "[ 20 ]"
        link[e189] "Rietveld"
        link[e190] "Gerrit Rietveld"
        link[e191] "[ 21 ]"
        link[e192] "[ 22 ]"
    region
      heading "Dropbox"
      link[e193] "edit"
      paragraph "In January 2013, Van Rossum started working at the cloud file storage company Dropbox . [ 23 ] [ 24 ]"
        link[e194] "cloud file storage"
        link[e195] "Dropbox"
        link[e196] "[ 23 ]"
        link[e197] "[ 24 ]"
      paragraph "In October 2019, Van Rossum left Dropbox and officially retired. [ 25 ] [ 26 ] [ 27 ]"
        link[e198] "[ 25 ]"
        link[e199] "[ 26 ]"
        link[e200] "[ 27 ]"
    region
      heading "Microsoft"
      link[e201] "edit"
      paragraph "On 12 November 2020 Van Rossum announced that he was coming out of retirement to join the Developer Division at Microsoft . He currently holds the title Distinguished Engineer at Microsoft. [ 28 ] [ 29 ] [ 30 ]"
        link[e202] "Microsoft"
        link[e203] "[ 28 ]"
        link[e204] "[ 29 ]"
        link[e205] "[ 30 ]"
  region
    heading "Python"
    link[e206] "edit"
    note "Main article: Python (programming language)"
      link[e207] "Python (programming language)"
    paragraph "In December 1989, Van Rossum had been looking for a " ' hobby' programming project that would keep [him] occupied during the week around Christmas" as his office was closed when he decided to write an interpreter for a "new scripting language [he] had been thinking about lately: a descendant of ABC…"
      link[e208] "interpreter"
      link[e209] "ABC"
      link[e210] "Unix"
      link[e211] "C"
      link[e212] "Monty Python's Flying Circus"
    paragraph "He has explained that Python's predecessor, ABC , was inspired by SETL , noting that ABC co-developer Lambert Meertens had "spent a year with the SETL group at NYU before coming up with the final ABC design". [ 31 ]"
      link[e213] "ABC"
      link[e214] "SETL"
      link[e215] "Lambert Meertens"
      link[e216] "NYU"
      link[e217] "[ 31 ]"
    paragraph "On 12 July 2018, Van Rossum announced that he would be stepping down from the position of benevolent dictator for life of the Python programming language. [ 32 ]"
      link[e218] "benevolent dictator for life"
      link[e219] "[ 32 ]"
  region
    heading ""Computer Programming for Everybody" proposal"
    link[e220] "edit"
    paragraph "In 1999, Van Rossum submitted a funding proposal to the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA) called "Computer Programming for Everybody", in which he further defined his goals for Python:"
      link[e221] "Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency"
    list
      listitem "An easy and intuitive language just as powerful as major competitors"
      listitem "Open source , so anyone can contribute to its development"
        link[e222] "Open source"
      listitem "Code that is as understandable as plain English"
      listitem "Suitability for everyday tasks, allowing for short development times"
    paragraph "In 2019, Python became the second most popular language on GitHub , the largest source code management website on the internet, after JavaScript . [ 33 ] In 2024 Python became the most used language on GitHub , overtaking JavaScript after a 10-year run as the most used language. [ 34 ] According to…"
      link[e223] "GitHub"
      link[e224] "source code"
      link[e225] "JavaScript"
      link[e226] "[ 33 ]"
      link[e227] "GitHub"
      link[e228] "JavaScript"
      link[e229] "[ 34 ]"
      link[e230] "[ 35 ]"
      link[e231] "TIOBE Programming Community Index"
      link[e232] "[ 36 ]"
  region
    heading "Awards"
    link[e233] "edit"
    list
      listitem "At the 2002 FOSDEM conference in Brussels , Van Rossum received the 2001 Award for the Advancement of Free Software from the Free Software Foundation (FSF) for his work on Python."
        link[e234] "FOSDEM"
        link[e235] "Brussels"
        link[e236] "Award for the Advancement of Free Software"
        link[e237] "Free Software Foundation"
      listitem "In May 2003, he received a NLUUG Award. [ 37 ]"
        link[e238] "NLUUG"
        link[e239] "[ 37 ]"
      listitem "In 2006, he was recognized as an ACM Distinguished Engineer ."
        link[e240] "ACM Distinguished Engineer"
      listitem "In 2018, he was made a Fellow of the Computer History museum . [ 38 ]"
        link[e241] "Computer History museum"
        link[e242] "[ 38 ]"
      listitem "In 2019, he was awarded the honorary title of Dijkstra Fellow by CWI . [ 39 ]"
        link[e243] "CWI"
        link[e244] "[ 39 ]"
      listitem "In 2023, he was awarded the C&C Prize by NEC Corporation for developing the Python programming language. [ 40 ]"
        link[e245] "C&C Prize"
        link[e246] "[ 40 ]"
  region

    paragraph "Guido van Rossum ( .mw-parser-output .IPA-label-small{font-size:85%}.mw-parser-output .references .IPA-label-small,.mw-parser-output .infobox .IPA-label-small,.mw-parser-output .navbox .IPA-label-small{font-size:100%} Dutch: [ ˈxidoː vɑn ˈrɔsʏm ] ; born 31 January 1956) is a Dutch programmer .   region
    heading "Life and education"
    link[e149] "edit"
    paragraph "Van Rossum was born and raised in the Netherlands , where he received a master's degree in mathematics and computer science from the University of Amsterdam in 1982. He received a bronze medal in 1974 in the International Mathematical Olympiad . [ 7 ] His brother, Just van Rossum , is a type design…"
      link[e150] "Netherlands"
      link[e151] "computer science"
      link[e152] "University of Amsterdam"
      link[e153] "International Mathematical Olympiad"
      link[e154] "[ 7 ]"
      link[e155] "Just van Rossum"
      link[e156] "type designer"
      link[e157] "typeface"
      link[e158] "[ 8 ]"
    paragraph "Van Rossum lives in Belmont , California, with his wife, Kim Knapp, [ 9 ] and their son. [ 10 ] [ 11 ] [ 12 ]"
      link[e159] "Belmont"
      link[e160] "[ 9 ]"
      link[e161] "[ 10 ]"
      link[e162] "[ 11 ]"
      link[e163] "[ 12 ]"
  region
    heading "Work"
    link[e164] "edit"
    region
      heading "Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica"
      link[e165] "edit"
      paragraph "While working at the Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica (CWI), Van Rossum wrote and contributed a glob() routine to BSD Unix in 1986 [ 13 ] [ 14 ] and helped develop the ABC programming language . He once stated, "I try to mention ABC's influence because I'm indebted to everything I learned during that…"
        link[e166] "Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica"
        link[e167] "glob()"
        link[e168] "BSD"
        link[e169] "Unix"
        link[e170] "[ 13 ]"
        link[e171] "[ 14 ]"
        link[e172] "ABC programming language"
        link[e173] "[ 15 ]"
        link[e174] "Grail"
        link[e175] "web browser"
        link[e176] "Python"
        link[e177] "HTML"
        link[e178] "[ 16 ]"
      paragraph "He has worked for various research institutes, including the CWI in the Netherlands, the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), and the Corporation for National Research Initiatives (CNRI). In May 2000, he left CNRI along with three other Python core developers to work for tech…"
        link[e179] "National Institute of Standards and Technology"
        link[e180] "Corporation for National Research Initiatives"
        link[e181] "[ 17 ]"
        link[e182] "[ 18 ]"
        link[e183] "Zope"
        link[e184] "[ 19 ]"
    region
      heading "Google"
      link[e185] "edit"
      paragraph "From 2005 to December 2012, Van Rossum worked at Google, where he spent half of his time developing the Python language. At Google, he developed Mondrian, a web-based code review system written in Python and used within the company. He named the software after the Dutch painter Piet Mondrian . [ 20…"
        link[e186] "code review"
        link[e187] "Piet Mondrian"
        link[e188] "[ 20 ]"
        link[e189] "Rietveld"
        link[e190] "Gerrit Rietveld"
        link[e191] "[ 21 ]"
        link[e192] "[ 22 ]"
    region
      heading "Dropbox"
      link[e193] "edit"
      paragraph "In January 2013, Van Rossum started working at the cloud file storage company Dropbox . [ 23 ] [ 24 ]"
        link[e194] "cloud file storage"
        link[e195] "Dropbox"
        link[e196] "[ 23 ]"
        link[e197] "[ 24 ]"
      paragraph "In October 2019, Van Rossum left Dropbox and officially retired. [ 25 ] [ 26 ] [ 27 ]"
        link[e198] "[ 25 ]"
        link[e199] "[ 26 ]"
        link[e200] "[ 27 ]"
    region
      heading "Microsoft"
      link[e201] "edit"
      paragraph "On 12 November 2020 Van Rossum announced that he was coming out of retirement to join the Developer Division at Microsoft . He currently holds the title Distinguished Engineer at Microsoft. [ 28 ] [ 29 ] [ 30 ]"
        link[e202] "Microsoft"
        link[e203] "[ 28 ]"
        link[e204] "[ 29 ]"
        link[e205] "[ 30 ]"
  region
    heading "Python"
    link[e206] "edit"
    note "Main article: Python (programming language)"
      link[e207] "Python (programming language)"
    paragraph "In December 1989, Van Rossum had been looking for a " ' hobby' programming project that would keep [him] occupied during the week around Christmas" as his office was closed when he decided to write an interpreter for a "new scripting language [he] had been thinking about lately: a descendant of ABC…"
      link[e208] "interpreter"
      link[e209] "ABC"
      link[e210] "Unix"
      link[e211] "C"
      link[e212] "Monty Python's Flying Circus"
    paragraph "He has explained that Python's predecessor, ABC , was inspired by SETL , noting that ABC co-developer Lambert Meertens had "spent a year with the SETL group at NYU before coming up with the final ABC design". [ 31 ]"
      link[e213] "ABC"
      link[e214] "SETL"
      link[e215] "Lambert Meertens"
      link[e216] "NYU"
      link[e217] "[ 31 ]"
    paragraph "On 12 July 2018, Van Rossum announced that he would be stepping down from the position of benevolent dictator for life of the Python programming language. [ 32 ]"
      link[e218] "benevolent dictator for life"
      link[e219] "[ 32 ]"
  region
    heading ""Computer Programming for Everybody" proposal"
    link[e220] "edit"
    paragraph "In 1999, Van Rossum submitted a funding proposal to the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA) called "Computer Programming for Everybody", in which he further defined his goals for Python:"
      link[e221] "Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency"
    list
      listitem "An easy and intuitive language just as powerful as major competitors"
      listitem "Open source , so anyone can contribute to its development"
        link[e222] "Open source"
      listitem "Code that is as understandable as plain English"
      listitem "Suitability for everyday tasks, allowing for short development times"
    paragraph "In 2019, Python became the second most popular language on GitHub , the largest source code management website on the internet, after JavaScript . [ 33 ] In 2024 Python became the most used language on GitHub , overtaking JavaScript after a 10-year run as the most used language. [ 34 ] According to…"
      link[e223] "GitHub"
      link[e224] "source code"
      link[e225] "JavaScript"
      link[e226] "[ 33 ]"
      link[e227] "GitHub"
      link[e228] "JavaScript"
      link[e229] "[ 34 ]"
      link[e230] "[ 35 ]"
      link[e231] "TIOBE Programming Community Index"
      link[e232] "[ 36 ]"
  region
    heading "Awards"
    link[e233] "edit"
    list
      listitem "At the 2002 FOSDEM conference in Brussels , Van Rossum received the 2001 Award for the Advancement of Free Software from the Free Software Foundation (FSF) for his work on Python."
        link[e234] "FOSDEM"
        link[e235] "Brussels"
        link[e236] "Award for the Advancement of Free Software"
        link[e237] "Free Software Foundation"
      listitem "In May 2003, he received a NLUUG Award. [ 37 ]"
        link[e238] "NLUUG"
        link[e239] "[ 37 ]"
      listitem "In 2006, he was recognized as an ACM Distinguished Engineer ."
        link[e240] "ACM Distinguished Engineer"
      listitem "In 2018, he was made a Fellow of the Computer History museum . [ 38 ]"
        link[e241] "Computer History museum"
        link[e242] "[ 38 ]"
      listitem "In 2019, he was awarded the honorary title of Dijkstra Fellow by CWI . [ 39 ]"
        link[e243] "CWI"
        link[e244] "[ 39 ]"
      listitem "In 2023, he was awarded the C&C Prize by NEC Corporation for developing the Python programming language. [ 40 ]"
        link[e245] "C&C Prize"
        link[e246] "[ 40 ]"

    paragraph "Guido van Rossum ( .mw-parser-output .IPA-label-small{font-size:85%}.mw-parser-output .references .IPA-label-small,.mw-parser-output .infobox .IPA-label-small,.mw-parser-output .navbox .IPA-label-small{font-size:100%} Dutch: [ ˈxidoː vɑn ˈrɔsʏm ] ; born 31 January 1956) is a Dutch programmer . He i…"

  region
    heading "Life and education"
    link[e149] "edit"
    paragraph "Van Rossum was born and raised in the Netherlands , where he received a master's degree in mathematics and computer science from the University of Amsterdam in 1982. He received a bronze medal in 1974 in the International Mathematical Olympiad . [ 7 ] His brother, Just van Rossum , is a type design…"
      link[e150] "Netherlands"
      link[e151] "computer science"
      link[e152] "University of Amsterdam"
      link[e153] "International Mathematical Olympiad"
      link[e154] "[ 7 ]"
      link[e155] "Just van Rossum"
      link[e156] "type designer"
      link[e157] "typeface"
      link[e158] "[ 8 ]"
    paragraph "Van Rossum lives in Belmont , California, with his wife, Kim Knapp, [ 9 ] and their son. [ 10 ] [ 11 ] [ 12 ]"
      link[e159] "Belmont"
      link[e160] "[ 9 ]"
      link[e161] "[ 10 ]"
      link[e162] "[ 11 ]"
      link[e163] "[ 12 ]"
  region
    heading "Work"
    link[e164] "edit"
    region
      heading "Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica"
      link[e165] "edit"
      paragraph "While working at the Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica (CWI), Van Rossum wrote and contributed a glob() routine to BSD Unix in 1986 [ 13 ] [ 14 ] and helped develop the ABC programming language . He once stated, "I try to mention ABC's influence because I'm indebted to everything I learned during that…"
        link[e166] "Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica"
        link[e167] "glob()"
        link[e168] "BSD"
        link[e169] "Unix"
        link[e170] "[ 13 ]"
        link[e171] "[ 14 ]"
        link[e172] "ABC programming language"
        link[e173] "[ 15 ]"
        link[e174] "Grail"
        link[e175] "web browser"
        link[e176] "Python"
        link[e177] "HTML"
        link[e178] "[ 16 ]"
      paragraph "He has worked for various research institutes, including the CWI in the Netherlands, the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), and the Corporation for National Research Initiatives (CNRI). In May 2000, he left CNRI along with three other Python core developers to work for tech…"
        link[e179] "National Institute of Standards and Technology"
        link[e180] "Corporation for National Research Initiatives"
        link[e181] "[ 17 ]"
        link[e182] "[ 18 ]"
        link[e183] "Zope"
        link[e184] "[ 19 ]"
    region
      heading "Google"
      link[e185] "edit"
      paragraph "From 2005 to December 2012, Van Rossum worked at Google, where he spent half of his time developing the Python language. At Google, he developed Mondrian, a web-based code review system written in Python and used within the company. He named the software after the Dutch painter Piet Mondrian . [ 20…"
        link[e186] "code review"
        link[e187] "Piet Mondrian"
        link[e188] "[ 20 ]"
        link[e189] "Rietveld"
        link[e190] "Gerrit Rietveld"
        link[e191] "[ 21 ]"
        link[e192] "[ 22 ]"
    region
      heading "Dropbox"
      link[e193] "edit"
      paragraph "In January 2013, Van Rossum started working at the cloud file storage company Dropbox . [ 23 ] [ 24 ]"
        link[e194] "cloud file storage"
        link[e195] "Dropbox"
        link[e196] "[ 23 ]"
        link[e197] "[ 24 ]"
      paragraph "In October 2019, Van Rossum left Dropbox and officially retired. [ 25 ] [ 26 ] [ 27 ]"
        link[e198] "[ 25 ]"
        link[e199] "[ 26 ]"
        link[e200] "[ 27 ]"
    region
      heading "Microsoft"
      link[e201] "edit"
      paragraph "On 12 November 2020 Van Rossum announced that he was coming out of retirement to join the Developer Division at Microsoft . He currently holds the title Distinguished Engineer at Microsoft. [ 28 ] [ 29 ] [ 30 ]"
        link[e202] "Microsoft"
        link[e203] "[ 28 ]"
        link[e204] "[ 29 ]"
        link[e205] "[ 30 ]"
  region
    heading "Python"
    link[e206] "edit"
    note "Main article: Python (programming language)"
      link[e207] "Python (programming language)"
    paragraph "In December 1989, Van Rossum had been looking for a " ' hobby' programming project that would keep [him] occupied during the week around Christmas" as his office was closed when he decided to write an interpreter for a "new scripting language [he] had been thinking about lately: a descendant of ABC…"
      link[e208] "interpreter"
      link[e209] "ABC"
      link[e210] "Unix"
      link[e211] "C"
      link[e212] "Monty Python's Flying Circus"
    paragraph "He has explained that Python's predecessor, ABC , was inspired by SETL , noting that ABC co-developer Lambert Meertens had "spent a year with the SETL group at NYU before coming up with the final ABC design". [ 31 ]"
      link[e213] "ABC"
      link[e214] "SETL"
      link[e215] "Lambert Meertens"
      link[e216] "NYU"
      link[e217] "[ 31 ]"
    paragraph "On 12 July 2018, Van Rossum announced that he would be stepping down from the position of benevolent dictator for life of the Python programming language. [ 32 ]"
      link[e218] "benevolent dictator for life"
      link[e219] "[ 32 ]"
  region
    heading ""Computer Programming for Everybody" proposal"
    link[e220] "edit"
    paragraph "In 1999, Van Rossum submitted a funding proposal to the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA) called "Computer Programming for Everybody", in which he further defined his goals for Python:"
      link[e221] "Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency"
    list
      listitem "An easy and intuitive language just as powerful as major competitors"
      listitem "Open source , so anyone can contribute to its development"
        link[e222] "Open source"
      listitem "Code that is as understandable as plain English"
      listitem "Suitability for everyday tasks, allowing for short development times"
    paragraph "In 2019, Python became the second most popular language on GitHub , the largest source code management website on the internet, after JavaScript . [ 33 ] In 2024 Python became the most used language on GitHub , overtaking JavaScript after a 10-year run as the most used language. [ 34 ] According to…"
      link[e223] "GitHub"
      link[e224] "source code"
      link[e225] "JavaScript"
      link[e226] "[ 33 ]"
      link[e227] "GitHub"
      link[e228] "JavaScript"
      link[e229] "[ 34 ]"
      link[e230] "[ 35 ]"
      link[e231] "TIOBE Programming Community Index"
      link[e232] "[ 36 ]"
  region
    heading "Awards"
    link[e233] "edit"
    list
      listitem "At the 2002 FOSDEM conference in Brussels , Van Rossum received the 2001 Award for the Advancement of Free Software from the Free Software Foundation (FSF) for his work on Python."
        link[e234] "FOSDEM"
        link[e235] "Brussels"
        link[e236] "Award for the Advancement of Free Software"
        link[e237] "Free Software Foundation"
      listitem "In May 2003, he received a NLUUG Award. [ 37 ]"
        link[e238] "NLUUG"
        link[e239] "[ 37 ]"
      listitem "In 2006, he was recognized as an ACM Distinguished Engineer ."
        link[e240] "ACM Distinguished Engineer"
      listitem "In 2018, he was made a Fellow of the Computer History museum . [ 38 ]"
        link[e241] "Computer History museum"
        link[e242] "[ 38 ]"
      listitem "In 2019, he was awarded the honorary title of Dijkstra Fellow by CWI . [ 39 ]"
        link[e243] "CWI"
        link[e244] "[ 39 ]"
      listitem "In 2023, he was awarded the C&C Prize by NEC Corporation for developing the Python programming language. [ 40 ]"
        link[e245] "C&C Prize"
        link[e246] "[ 40 ]"

      link[e155] "Just van Rossum"
      link[e156] "type designer"
      link[e157] "typeface"
      link[e158] "[ 8 ]"
    paragraph "Van Rossum lives in Belmont , California, with his wife, Kim Knapp, [ 9 ] and their son. [ 10 ] [ 11 ] [ 12 ]"
      link[e159] "Belmont"
      link[e160] "[ 9 ]"
      link[e161] "[ 10 ]"
      link[e162] "[ 11 ]"
      link[e163] "[ 12 ]"
  region
    heading "Work"
    link[e164] "edit"
    region
      heading "Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica"
      link[e165] "edit"
      paragraph "While working at the Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica (CWI), Van Rossum wrote and contributed a glob() routine to BSD Unix in 1986 [ 13 ] [ 14 ] and helped develop the ABC programming language . He once stated, "I try to mention ABC's influence because I'm indebted to everything I learned during that…"
        link[e166] "Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica"
        link[e167] "glob()"
        link[e168] "BSD"
        link[e169] "Unix"
        link[e170] "[ 13 ]"
        link[e171] "[ 14 ]"
        link[e172] "ABC programming language"
        link[e173] "[ 15 ]"
        link[e174] "Grail"
        link[e175] "web browser"
        link[e176] "Python"
        link[e177] "HTML"
        link[e178] "[ 16 ]"
      paragraph "He has worked for various research institutes, including the CWI in the Netherlands, the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), and the Corporation for National Research Initiatives (CNRI). In May 2000, he left CNRI along with three other Python core developers to work for tech…"
        link[e179] "National Institute of Standards and Technology"
        link[e180] "Corporation for National Research Initiatives"
        link[e181] "[ 17 ]"
        link[e182] "[ 18 ]"
        link[e183] "Zope"
        link[e184] "[ 19 ]"
    region
      heading "Google"
      link[e185] "edit"
      paragraph "From 2005 to December 2012, Van Rossum worked at Google, where he spent half of his time developing the Python language. At Google, he developed Mondrian, a web-based code review system written in Python and used within the company. He named the software after the Dutch painter Piet Mondrian . [ 20…"
        link[e186] "code review"
        link[e187] "Piet Mondrian"
        link[e188] "[ 20 ]"
        link[e189] "Rietveld"
        link[e190] "Gerrit Rietveld"
        link[e191] "[ 21 ]"
        link[e192] "[ 22 ]"
    region
      heading "Dropbox"
      link[e193] "edit"
      paragraph "In January 2013, Van Rossum started working at the cloud file storage company Dropbox . [ 23 ] [ 24 ]"
        link[e194] "cloud file storage"
        link[e195] "Dropbox"
        link[e196] "[ 23 ]"
        link[e197] "[ 24 ]"
      paragraph "In October 2019, Van Rossum left Dropbox and officially retired. [ 25 ] [ 26 ] [ 27 ]"
        link[e198] "[ 25 ]"
        link[e199] "[ 26 ]"
        link[e200] "[ 27 ]"
    region
      heading "Microsoft"
      link[e201] "edit"
      paragraph "On 12 November 2020 Van Rossum announced that he was coming out of retirement to join the Developer Division at Microsoft . He currently holds the title Distinguished Engineer at Microsoft. [ 28 ] [ 29 ] [ 30 ]"
        link[e202] "Microsoft"
        link[e203] "[ 28 ]"
        link[e204] "[ 29 ]"
        link[e205] "[ 30 ]"
  region
    heading "Python"
    link[e206] "edit"
    note "Main article: Python (programming language)"
      link[e207] "Python (programming language)"
    paragraph "In December 1989, Van Rossum had been looking for a " ' hobby' programming project that would keep [him] occupied during the week around Christmas" as his office was closed when he decided to write an interpreter for a "new scripting language [he] had been thinking about lately: a descendant of ABC…"
      link[e208] "interpreter"
      link[e209] "ABC"
      link[e210] "Unix"
      link[e211] "C"
      link[e212] "Monty Python's Flying Circus"
    paragraph "He has explained that Python's predecessor, ABC , was inspired by SETL , noting that ABC co-developer Lambert Meertens had "spent a year with the SETL group at NYU before coming up with the final ABC design". [ 31 ]"
      link[e213] "ABC"
      link[e214] "SETL"
      link[e215] "Lambert Meertens"
      link[e216] "NYU"
      link[e217] "[ 31 ]"
    paragraph "On 12 July 2018, Van Rossum announced that he would be stepping down from the position of benevolent dictator for life of the Python programming language. [ 32 ]"
      link[e218] "benevolent dictator for life"
      link[e219] "[ 32 ]"
  region
    heading ""Computer Programming for Everybody" proposal"
    link[e220] "edit"
    paragraph "In 1999, Van Rossum submitted a funding proposal to the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA) called "Computer Programming for Everybody", in which he further defined his goals for Python:"
      link[e221] "Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency"
    list
      listitem "An easy and intuitive language just as powerful as major competitors"
      listitem "Open source , so anyone can contribute to its development"
        link[e222] "Open source"
      listitem "Code that is as understandable as plain English"
      listitem "Suitability for everyday tasks, allowing for short development times"
    paragraph "In 2019, Python became the second most popular language on GitHub , the largest source code management website on the internet, after JavaScript . [ 33 ] In 2024 Python became the most used language on GitHub , overtaking JavaScript after a 10-year run as the most used language. [ 34 ] According to…"
      link[e223] "GitHub"
      link[e224] "source code"
      link[e225] "JavaScript"
      link[e226] "[ 33 ]"
      link[e227] "GitHub"
      link[e228] "JavaScript"
      link[e229] "[ 34 ]"
      link[e230] "[ 35 ]"
      link[e231] "TIOBE Programming Community Index"
      link[e232] "[ 36 ]"
  region
    heading "Awards"
    link[e233] "edit"
    list
      listitem "At the 2002 FOSDEM conference in Brussels , Van Rossum received the 2001 Award for the Advancement of Free Software from the Free Software Foundation (FSF) for his work on Python."
        link[e234] "FOSDEM"
        link[e235] "Brussels"
        link[e236] "Award for the Advancement of Free Software"
        link[e237] "Free Software Foundation"
      listitem "In May 2003, he received a NLUUG Award. [ 37 ]"
        link[e238] "NLUUG"
        link[e239] "[ 37 ]"
      listitem "In 2006, he was recognized as an ACM Distinguished Engineer ."
        link[e240] "ACM Distinguished Engineer"
      listitem "In 2018, he was made a Fellow of the Computer History museum . [ 38 ]"
        link[e241] "Computer History museum"
        link[e242] "[ 38 ]"
      listitem "In 2019, he was awarded the honorary title of Dijkstra Fellow by CWI . [ 39 ]"
        link[e243] "CWI"
        link[e244] "[ 39 ]"
      listitem "In 2023, he was awarded the C&C Prize by NEC Corporation for developing the Python programming language. [ 40 ]"
        link[e245] "C&C Prize"
        link[e246] "[ 40 ]"

  region
    heading "Life and education"
    link[e149] "edit"
    paragraph "Van Rossum was born and raised in the Netherlands , where he received a master's degree in mathematics and computer science from the University of Amsterdam in 1982. He received a bronze medal in 1974 in the International Mathematical Olympiad . [ 7 ] His brother, Just van Rossum , is a type design…"
      link[e150] "Netherlands"
      link[e151] "computer science"
      link[e152] "University of Amsterdam"
      link[e153] "International Mathematical Olympiad"
      link[e154] "[ 7 ]"
      link[e155] "Just van Rossum"
      link[e156] "type designer"
      link[e157] "typeface"
      link[e158] "[ 8 ]"
    paragraph "Van Rossum lives in Belmont , California, with his wife, Kim Knapp, [ 9 ] and their son. [ 10 ] [ 11 ] [ 12 ]"
      link[e159] "Belmont"
      link[e160] "[ 9 ]"
      link[e161] "[ 10 ]"
      link[e162] "[ 11 ]"
      link[e163] "[ 12 ]"
  region
    heading "Work"
    link[e164] "edit"
    region
      heading "Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica"
      link[e165] "edit"
      paragraph "While working at the Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica (CWI), Van Rossum wrote and contributed a glob() routine to BSD Unix in 1986 [ 13 ] [ 14 ] and helped develop the ABC programming language . He once stated, "I try to mention ABC's influence because I'm indebted to everything I learned during that…"
        link[e166] "Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica"
        link[e167] "glob()"
        link[e168] "BSD"
        link[e169] "Unix"
        link[e170] "[ 13 ]"
        link[e171] "[ 14 ]"
        link[e172] "ABC programming language"
        link[e173] "[ 15 ]"
        link[e174] "Grail"
        link[e175] "web browser"
        link[e176] "Python"
        link[e177] "HTML"
        link[e178] "[ 16 ]"
      paragraph "He has worked for various research institutes, including the CWI in the Netherlands, the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), and the Corporation for National Research Initiatives (CNRI). In May 2000, he left CNRI along with three other Python core developers to work for tech…"
        link[e179] "National Institute of Standards and Technology"
        link[e180] "Corporation for National Research Initiatives"
        link[e181] "[ 17 ]"
        link[e182] "[ 18 ]"
        link[e183] "Zope"
        link[e184] "[ 19 ]"
    region
      heading "Google"
      link[e185] "edit"
      paragraph "From 2005 to December 2012, Van Rossum worked at Google, where he spent half of his time developing the Python language. At Google, he developed Mondrian, a web-based code review system written in Python and used within the company. He named the software after the Dutch painter Piet Mondrian . [ 20…"
        link[e186] "code review"
        link[e187] "Piet Mondrian"
        link[e188] "[ 20 ]"
        link[e189] "Rietveld"
        link[e190] "Gerrit Rietveld"
        link[e191] "[ 21 ]"
        link[e192] "[ 22 ]"
    region
      heading "Dropbox"
      link[e193] "edit"
      paragraph "In January 2013, Van Rossum started working at the cloud file storage company Dropbox . [ 23 ] [ 24 ]"
        link[e194] "cloud file storage"
        link[e195] "Dropbox"
        link[e196] "[ 23 ]"
        link[e197] "[ 24 ]"
      paragraph "In October 2019, Van Rossum left Dropbox and officially retired. [ 25 ] [ 26 ] [ 27 ]"
        link[e198] "[ 25 ]"
        link[e199] "[ 26 ]"
        link[e200] "[ 27 ]"
    region
      heading "Microsoft"
      link[e201] "edit"
      paragraph "On 12 November 2020 Van Rossum announced that he was coming out of retirement to join the Developer Division at Microsoft . He currently holds the title Distinguished Engineer at Microsoft. [ 28 ] [ 29 ] [ 30 ]"
        link[e202] "Microsoft"
        link[e203] "[ 28 ]"
        link[e204] "[ 29 ]"
        link[e205] "[ 30 ]"
  region
    heading "Python"
    link[e206] "edit"
    note "Main article: Python (programming language)"
      link[e207] "Python (programming language)"
    paragraph "In December 1989, Van Rossum had been looking for a " ' hobby' programming project that would keep [him] occupied during the week around Christmas" as his office was closed when he decided to write an interpreter for a "new scripting language [he] had been thinking about lately: a descendant of ABC…"
      link[e208] "interpreter"
      link[e209] "ABC"
      link[e210] "Unix"
      link[e211] "C"
      link[e212] "Monty Python's Flying Circus"
    paragraph "He has explained that Python's predecessor, ABC , was inspired by SETL , noting that ABC co-developer Lambert Meertens had "spent a year with the SETL group at NYU before coming up with the final ABC design". [ 31 ]"
      link[e213] "ABC"
      link[e214] "SETL"
      link[e215] "Lambert Meertens"
      link[e216] "NYU"
      link[e217] "[ 31 ]"
    paragraph "On 12 July 2018, Van Rossum announced that he would be stepping down from the position of benevolent dictator for life of the Python programming language. [ 32 ]"
      link[e218] "benevolent dictator for life"
      link[e219] "[ 32 ]"
  region
    heading ""Computer Programming for Everybody" proposal"
    link[e220] "edit"
    paragraph "In 1999, Van Rossum submitted a funding proposal to the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA) called "Computer Programming for Everybody", in which he further defined his goals for Python:"
      link[e221] "Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency"
    list
      listitem "An easy and intuitive language just as powerful as major competitors"
      listitem "Open source , so anyone can contribute to its development"
        link[e222] "Open source"
      listitem "Code that is as understandable as plain English"
      listitem "Suitability for everyday tasks, allowing for short development times"
    paragraph "In 2019, Python became the second most popular language on GitHub , the largest source code management website on the internet, after JavaScript . [ 33 ] In 2024 Python became the most used language on GitHub , overtaking JavaScript after a 10-year run as the most used language. [ 34 ] According to…"
